---
title: "Critical Slowing Down in Infectious Disease Systems"
subtitle: "Early warning signals before epidemic transitions"
author: "John Drake"
institute: "Odum School of Ecology, University of Georgia"
event: "Annual Meeting of the Ecological Society of America"
location: "Portland, Oregon"
date: today
format:
  drake-navy-revealjs:
    slide-number: c/t
    transition: none
    incremental: false
---

# Motivation {.divider}

## The forecasting challenge

Can we detect when a disease system is approaching a critical
transition --- before the transition occurs?

This question sits at the intersection of dynamical systems theory
and public health surveillance.

::: {.fragment}
The answer involves a phenomenon physicists call
*critical slowing down*.
:::

## What is critical slowing down?

Near a bifurcation point, the dominant eigenvalue of the system
approaches zero. This produces measurable signatures:

- Increased **autocorrelation** in time series
- Rising **variance** in case counts
- Slower **return to equilibrium** after perturbations

### These signals precede the transition itself

## A canonical model {.figure-slide}

![](figures/bifurcation-diagram.png){fig-align="center"}

<p class="caption">Bifurcation diagram for the SIS model as transmissibility increases through $R_0 = 1$.</p>

## The SIS model

The simplest framework exhibiting a transcritical bifurcation:

$$
\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta S I - \gamma I
$$

At $R_0 = \beta / \gamma = 1$, the disease-free equilibrium loses
stability and an endemic equilibrium emerges.

## Dreissenid mussel invasions {.figure}

![](figures/fig-invasion-curves-1.png){fig-align="center"}

<p class="caption">Newly invaded lakes (left) and cumulative invaded lakes (right) for *D. polymorpha* and *D. bugensis* in North America.</p>

# Methods {.transition}

From theory to detection

## Detecting the signal in data

```r
library(earlywarnings)

# Compute rolling window statistics
ews <- generic_ews(
  timeseries = cases,
  winsize    = 50,
  detrending = "gaussian",
  bandwidth  = 10
)
```

## Key results

| Indicator       | Lead time | Sensitivity |
|-----------------|:---------:|:-----------:|
| Autocorrelation | Longer    | Moderate    |
| Variance        | Shorter   | Moderate    |
| Return rate     | Longer    | Higher      |
| Combined index  | Longer    | Highest     |

### Qualitative pattern --- specific values depend on system and detection method

## A field perspective {background-image="figures/field.jpg" background-size="cover"}

::: {.frosted}
Long-term surveillance sites provide the high-frequency data
needed to estimate early warning statistics in natural disease
systems.
:::

## A reflection {.divider}

> The goal is not to predict the future, but to make
> the present more legible.

## Implications

These methods have practical value for:

1. **Surveillance systems** --- triggering enhanced monitoring
2. **Vaccination policy** --- timing booster campaigns
3. **Elimination programs** --- confirming approach to zero

::: {.fragment}
The statistical machinery is straightforward; the hard part is
building surveillance systems that generate data at the right
resolution.
:::

## Collaborators {.collaborators}

::: {.people}

::: {.person}
![](figures/person1.png)

Andrew Park
:::

::: {.person}
![](figures/person2.png)

Pejman Rohani
:::

::: {.person}
![](figures/person3.png)

Suzanne O'Regan
:::

::: {.person}
![](figures/person4.png)

Bogdan Epureanu
:::

:::

## {.full-image background-image="figures/photo.png" background-size="cover" background-position="center"}

::: {.full-image-label}
Full image slide
:::

## Institutional context {.watermark background-image="figures/watermark.png" background-size="contain" background-position="center" background-opacity="0.12"}

This work is supported by the University of Georgia and the
Center for the Ecology of Infectious Diseases.

Watermark slides are useful for institutional branding or
subtle visual identity on acknowledgment and closing slides.

# Thank you {.divider}

*jdrake@uga.edu*
